🚦 Authorities crack down on ransomware, a crypto fraud ring, and DPRK IT worker schemes, ‘SleepyDuck’ malware uses Ethereum smart contracts to evade takedowns, and Iran-linked attackers target U.S. policy experts with phishing and espionage campaigns. 🚦

This is the week in cyber. This is SentinelOne’s Good, Bad, and Ugly cyber news roundup. ⬇️

✅ GOOD: Global law enforcement indicted malicious insiders, sanctioned North Korean banks for laundering ill-gotten crypto, and arrested nine suspects in a wide-spread crypto fraud network.
• Three ex-cybersecurity employees were officially indicted for their role in BlackCat ransomware ops, stealing data and demanding ransom payouts. 
• OFAC sanctioned two North Korean banks and eight associates for laundering cryptocurrency stolen via various IT worker scams. 
• Eurojust authorities arrested nine people involved in a cryptocurrency fraud ring that has stolen more than €600 million ($689 million) across multiple countries.

⚠️ BAD: New RAT dubbed ‘SleepyDuck’ is masquerading as a Solidity extension on Open VSX open-source registry, garnering over 53,000 downloads since its upload on October 31. 
• The malware uses Ethereum smart contracts to manage its C2 communications in order to establish persistence, even if a takedown of the main server occurs. 
• Open VSX is working with VS Code to block emerging threats, and has pushed out new security measures such as shorter token lifetimes, automated scans, and revoking leaked credentials faster.

🤢 UGLY: Iran-based threat cluster called ‘UNK_SmudgedSerpent’ continue to target various U.S. academics, foreign policy experts, and think tanks via phishing and spoofing.
• The newly-discovered cluster uses politically-themed lures, related to the current tension between Iran and Israel, to tempt their victims into clicking on malicious links.
• The links take victims to spoofed Microsoft login pages, allowing the attackers to harvest credentials and potentially gather Western insights on regional policies and strategic technology.

🚦 OpenAI Disrupts State-Linked AI Abuse, UK Police Arrest Teens in Nursery Hack, New “Crimson Collective” Threat Actor Emerges, and Discord Breach Exposes Millions 🚦

This is the week in cyber. This is SentinelOne’s Good, Bad, and Ugly cyber news roundup. ⬇️

✅ GOOD: UK arrests teens over nursery hack & OpenAI disrupts AI-abuse by state-linked actors
• OpenAI says it has disrupted three malicious clusters from Russian, North Korean, and Chinese actors misusing ChatGPT to aid in malware development, phishing, and other cyberattack tactics. 
• UK’s Metropolitan Police arrested two teenagers in connection with a ransomware/data theft attack on a nursery chain where photos, names, and addresses of 8,000 children were stolen. 

⚠️ BAD: New threat actor “Crimson Collective” observed scaling cloud attacks
• Cyber researchers have identified Crimson Collective, a newly observed group conducting covert attacks in cloud environments, compromising long-lived credentials, creating privilege escalations, and exfiltrating data.
• They claimed responsibility for stealing ~570 GB from Red Hat’s GitLab repositories recently and appear to be partnering with known leak/collective groups. 

🤢 UGLY: Hackers claim Discord breach exposed personal data of 5.5 million users
• Hackers claim they stole user data from Discord’s support system vendor, Zendesk, including government-ID photos, emails, IPs, usernames, and partial payment data.
• Discord counters that it was a third-party support provider compromise, not a direct breach, and says only ~70,000 users may have had ID photos exposed.

What leaders should do: Quarantine inbound .js file downloads, tighten attachment detonation policies, block initial callback domains, and train for “fake voicemail” lure patterns.

🤢 UGLY
Salt Typhoon Scale and Persistence: A China-linked cluster is maintaining long-term access by exploiting known router flaws (Cisco IOS XE CVE-2023-20198/20273, Cisco CVE-2018-0171; Ivanti CVE-2024-21887; PAN-OS CVE-2024-3400) to pivot across telecoms and into government, transportation, lodging, and military networks.Activity has observed worldwide across 80 countries since at least 2021. 

What leaders should do (today):
✅ Patch/validate edge devices listed in the advisory;
✅ Hunt for ACL tampering, non-standard SSH ports, GRE/IPsec tunnels, and traffic mirroring;
